title: Reproducible Research Using R: Student Work in Practice
text:
Reproducible Research in Practice
This volume represents a collection of student portfolio books developed as part of a graduate course in Reproducible Research using R.
Throughout the semester, students completed a series of assignments using the programming language R. Rather than treating these as “one-and-done” submissions, each assignment was intentionally integrated into a larger body of work. Individual Quarto documents were progressively developed and combined into full Quarto books, allowing students to document their learning and analytical development over time.
The structure for these portfolio books—along with the assignments that comprise them—is outlined in Chapter 13 of the Teaching Reproducible Research Using R, which focuses on transforming coursework into cohesive, reproducible portfolio artifacts.

title: Reproducible Research Using R: Student Work in Practice
text:
A Shared Framework, Individual Approaches
All students in this course completed the same sequence of assignments, covering key stages of the data analysis process:

Data import and cleaning

Data transformation and exploration

Statistical analysis (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA, regression)

Data visualization

Reproducible reporting using Quarto

While the structure was shared, the execution was not. Students were encouraged to approach questions from different angles, create visualizations using their own perspectives, and communicate findings in their own voice.






TipAs I always say:



“R puts the R in Artist”


Creativity and individuality were treated as essential components of the analytical process, not secondary to it.

title: Reproducible Research Using R: Student Work in Practice
text:
From Assignment to Artifact
A central goal of this course was to move beyond “one-and-done” assignments. Instead of producing work that is completed and discarded, students developed portfolios that serve as lasting artifacts of their learning.
Each portfolio included:

Fully reproducible analyses

Clearly documented code and outputs

Interpretable statistical results

Visualizations designed to communicate findings

A structured, publishable format

These portfolios are intended to be shared—with peers, instructors, and potential employers—as evidence of both technical skill and analytical thinking.

title: Reproducible Research Using R: Student Work in Practice
text:
Learning Through Reproducibility
Reproducibility was not treated as an afterthought, but as a core component of the learning process. By integrating code, results, and interpretation into a single workflow, students were able to:

Better understand their own analyses

Debug and refine their work more effectively

Communicate results with clarity and transparency

Build habits aligned with modern data science and research practices

This approach reflects a broader shift in how data analysis is taught and practiced—emphasizing not just results, but the process used to generate them.

title: Reproducible Research Using R: Student Work in Practice
text:
What This Volume Represents
This collection captures more than technical outcomes—it documents a range of learning experiences:

Students overcoming initial uncertainty with programming

The transition from copying code to understanding it

The development of independent problem-solving skills

The ability to translate data into meaningful insights

Each chapter introduces a student and provides an overview of their portfolio, along with a link to their full Quarto book. Readers are encouraged to explore these portfolios directly to see the complete analytical workflows in action.

title: Reproducible Research Using R: Student Work in Practice
text:
Looking Ahead
This volume represents the first iteration of a broader effort to document and publish student work in reproducible research. Future volumes will continue to build on this model, expanding the collection of publicly available student portfolios and highlighting evolving approaches to teaching and applying data analysis.

title: 2  Crystal Adote
text:
2.1 About the Author
Crystal Adote is a graduate student at Brooklyn College who developed a strong foundation in data analysis and reproducible research over the course of the semester. Entering the class with little prior experience in R, she built both technical skills and confidence through hands-on work with real datasets.

title: 2  Crystal Adote
text:
2.2 Learning Journey
Throughout the semester, Crystal gained experience working across the full data analysis pipeline. She learned how to import and clean datasets, perform statistical analyses, and communicate findings using reproducible workflows.
Her work includes applying a range of statistical techniques such as t-tests, chi-square tests, ANOVAs, linear regression, logistic regression, and correlation analysis. She also explored multiple R packages and developed an interactive Shiny application.
One area she initially found challenging was writing functions in R. However, with practice and persistence, she was able to build a stronger understanding of how functions work and how they can be used effectively in analysis.

title: 2  Crystal Adote
text:
2.3 Reflection & Growth
Crystal’s biggest takeaway from the course was the progress she made over time. Starting with little background in R, she is now able to write scripts that import, clean, and analyze real-world datasets.
Her confidence grew significantly throughout the semester, and she developed a strong set of practical skills that she can carry forward into future academic and professional work.

title: 2  Crystal Adote
text:
2.4 Skills Demonstrated

Data import and cleaning in R

Statistical analysis (t-tests, chi-square, ANOVA, regression)

Data exploration and interpretation

Reproducible research using Quarto

Introductory Shiny app development

title: 3  Jonah Dratfield
text:
3.1 About the Author
Jonah Dratfield is a graduate student at Brooklyn College who developed a strong foundation in statistical programming and data analysis through hands-on work in R. His portfolio reflects both his technical growth and his ability to connect data analysis to meaningful research questions.

title: 3  Jonah Dratfield
text:
3.2 Learning Journey
This portfolio showcases a series of assignments completed as part of a graduate course in reproducible research. Through this work, Jonah built experience using R to analyze a variety of datasets and to create clear, informative visualizations.
Beyond technical execution, his work highlights an evolving ability to approach data analytically—using statistical methods not just to produce results, but to better understand and interpret underlying patterns.

title: 3  Jonah Dratfield
text:
3.3 Reflection & Growth
At the start of the semester, Jonah had little experience working with R. Over time, he developed confidence not only in performing statistical analyses, but also in independently learning new techniques and troubleshooting challenges.
One of the biggest hurdles early on was navigating a new programming language and making sense of its structure. However, as the course progressed—particularly when working with R Markdown—he began to see how each part of the workflow connected to broader analytical questions. This shift allowed the process to “click,” transforming individual tasks into a cohesive analytical approach.
Jonah identifies as an idea-driven thinker, and this perspective became a strength in his work. By focusing on how each analysis addressed a specific question, he was able to deepen both his understanding and the quality of his interpretations.

title: 3  Jonah Dratfield
text:
3.4 Key Takeaways

Developed a strong foundational understanding of R

Built confidence in self-directed learning and problem-solving

Applied statistical analyses to generate meaningful insights from data

Strengthened ability to interpret and communicate nuanced findings

title: 3  Jonah Dratfield
text:
3.5 Looking Ahead
Jonah plans to continue using R in his thesis and future research projects. While his adviser primarily uses Excel and SPSS, he sees R as an opportunity to streamline analyses and expand the depth of his work.

title: 4  Joyce Escatel-Flores
text:
4.1 About the Author
Joyce is a graduate student at Brooklyn College who, over the course of the semester, transformed her perspective on coding and data analysis. Her work reflects both technical development and a significant increase in confidence as she learned to approach programming with curiosity rather than hesitation.

title: 4  Joyce Escatel-Flores
text:
4.2 Learning Journey
At the beginning of the course, Joyce was apprehensive about learning R due to a prior experience that made the language feel overwhelming and difficult to follow. This initial hesitation shaped her expectations, but as the semester progressed, she steadily built both her skills and her confidence.
Through hands-on assignments, Joyce learned how to analyze data in R, create clear and visually engaging graphs and tables, and develop reproducible workflows using Markdown. Beyond technical skills, the course helped reinforce her understanding of statistical concepts by making them more concrete and applicable.

title: 4  Joyce Escatel-Flores
text:
4.3 Reflection & Growth
One of the most meaningful changes for Joyce was the shift in how she viewed herself as a learner. What began as uncertainty evolved into confidence—she now feels capable of starting coding projects from scratch and continuing to develop her skills independently.
The coursework itself was challenging, often requiring significant time and effort to complete. As a self-described perfectionist, Joyce dedicated hours—sometimes days—to refining her assignments. However, this persistence paid off, helping her retain what she learned and deepen her understanding.
She is especially proud of her ability to create and publish Markdown documents, which serve as a tangible representation of her progress. Revisiting these projects allows her to see how far she has come over the course of the semester.

title: 4  Joyce Escatel-Flores
text:
4.4 Key Takeaways

Overcame initial fear of coding and built lasting confidence

Developed skills in data analysis, visualization, and reproducible workflows

Strengthened understanding of statistical concepts through applied work

Gained experience creating and publishing Markdown-based projects

title: 4  Joyce Escatel-Flores
text:
4.5 Looking Ahead
Joyce plans to continue practicing R beyond the course and hopes to further refine her work in future projects, including potential contributions to Open Data Week. She is motivated to keep building on the strong foundation she developed this semester.

“I feel more confident and not scared to start coding something from scratch on my own.”

title: 5  Robert Hutto
text:
5.1 About the Author
Rob is a graduate student at Brooklyn College who developed a structured and thoughtful approach to data analysis over the course of the semester. His work reflects a transition from fragmented coding practices to a more intentional, reproducible workflow in R.

title: 5  Robert Hutto
text:
5.2 Learning Journey
At the start of the course, Rob had some prior exposure to RStudio through independent research, but his approach lacked organization and clarity. Much of his work involved copying and pasting code—often with limited understanding of how individual components functioned—resulting in scripts that were difficult to interpret or maintain.
Through consistent practice and guided assignments, he developed a more systematic approach to working with data. His portfolio demonstrates the ability to import, clean, and prepare datasets from multiple sources, conduct statistical analyses such as t-tests, ANOVAs, and regression models, and create clear visualizations using tables and graphs.
Just as importantly, he learned to structure his work in a way that is readable and interpretable by others, allowing both himself and collaborators to better understand analytical workflows.

title: 5  Robert Hutto
text:
5.3 Reflection & Growth
One of Rob’s most significant areas of growth was moving from simply executing code to truly understanding it. This shift enabled him to build more organized, transparent, and reproducible analyses.
He also developed a strong ability to identify and communicate meaningful patterns within data—transforming raw datasets into clear, interpretable stories. This skill represents a key step beyond technical execution and into applied data analysis.
A recurring challenge throughout the course was managing expectations around assignments. Tasks that initially appeared straightforward often required significant time to debug and refine, reinforcing the importance of patience and persistence in programming.

title: 5  Robert Hutto
text:
5.4 Advice for Future Students

Don’t underestimate seemingly simple tasks. Budget extra time for troubleshooting. What looks like a 30-minute assignment can easily become a multiple-hour debugging session—even with the help of AI. That’s part of the learning process.

title: 5  Robert Hutto
text:
5.5 Key Takeaways

Transitioned from unstructured coding to organized, reproducible workflows

Developed proficiency in data cleaning, analysis, and visualization

Improved ability to read, understand, and communicate code

Strengthened skills in extracting and explaining insights from data

title: 7  Shannon Joyce
text:
7.1 About the Author
Shannon is a graduate student at Brooklyn College who developed a comprehensive skill set in data analysis and reproducible research over the course of the semester. Her work reflects a strong ability to move from raw data to meaningful insights using structured, reproducible workflows in R.

title: 7  Shannon Joyce
text:
7.2 Learning Journey
This portfolio is a compilation of assignments completed in a graduate course on Reproducible Psychological Research. Across these projects, Shannon demonstrates proficiency in writing and executing R scripts, cleaning and transforming real-world datasets, and conducting statistical analyses.
Her work spans the full data analysis pipeline—from data import and wrangling to modeling, visualization, and communication. She developed experience using tools from the :contentReferenceoaicite:0 to efficiently manipulate data, including filtering, grouping, summarizing, and joining datasets in preparation for analysis.

title: 7  Shannon Joyce
text:
7.3 Reflection & Growth
At the beginning of the course, Shannon had limited experience with R. Early efforts focused on building foundational skills and becoming comfortable working within the RStudio environment. By the end of the semester, she was able to independently complete full analytical workflows—moving from raw data to statistical modeling and clear interpretation.
A key area of growth was working with real datasets, which often required substantial cleaning and restructuring before analysis. Shannon developed skills in inspecting variables, recoding values, handling missing data, and reshaping datasets into formats suitable for analysis. These steps were essential in ensuring the accuracy and validity of her results.

title: 7  Shannon Joyce
text:
7.4 Analytical Skills & Techniques
Throughout the semester, Shannon gained experience applying a wide range of statistical methods, including:

t-tests for comparing group means

ANOVAs for analyzing differences across multiple groups

Correlation matrices for exploring relationships between variables

Regression models for prediction and explanation

She also learned to evaluate model outputs holistically, considering multiple variables and metrics rather than relying on a single statistic.

title: 7  Shannon Joyce
text:
7.5 Communication & Visualization
In addition to statistical analysis, Shannon strengthened her ability to communicate results clearly and effectively. She learned to translate statistical findings into plain language, connecting results back to the original research questions.
Her portfolio includes data visualizations created with :contentReferenceoaicite:1, as well as reproducible reports built using :contentReferenceoaicite:2. These tools allowed her to integrate code, output, and narrative into cohesive, shareable documents.
She also explored interactive data presentation through the development of a Shiny application.

title: 7  Shannon Joyce
text:
7.6 Key Takeaways

Built end-to-end data analysis workflows in R

Developed strong data cleaning and transformation skills

Applied a range of statistical and predictive modeling techniques

Strengthened ability to interpret and communicate results

Created reproducible reports and interactive applications

title: 7  Shannon Joyce
text:
7.7 Looking Ahead
Shannon now feels confident continuing to build on these skills in future academic and professional work. Her portfolio reflects both a strong technical foundation and the ability to apply data analysis to meaningful questions.

title: 8  Emma Tupone

Through hands-on projects, she built skills in manipulating and cleaning data, visualizing patterns, and applying statistical techniques to answer meaningful research questions.
She developed proficiency using :contentReferenceoaicite:1 to create clear and informative visualizations, and gained experience with statistical methods including t-tests, ANOVAs, chi-square tests, and logistic regression. Beyond execution, she strengthened her ability to interpret results thoughtfully and connect findings to real-world implications.

title: 8  Emma Tupone
text:
8.4 Challenges & Problem Solving
Emma encountered several challenges throughout the semester, including:

Debugging complex R code, particularly when working with large datasets or interactive components

Interpreting nuanced statistical outputs such as residuals, effect sizes, and model diagnostics

Synthesizing insights across multiple assignments into cohesive conclusions

Addressing these challenges required persistence, iterative testing, and careful attention to both coding and statistical reasoning.

title: 8  Emma Tupone
text:
8.5 Achievements
Emma is especially proud of completing her Bookdown portfolio, which integrates multiple assignments into a cohesive and polished body of work. This project reflects her ability to combine reproducible code, clear visualizations, and well-structured interpretations.
Notable accomplishments include:

Developing interactive visualizations using Shiny

Conducting advanced analyses such as logistic regression and chi-square contribution analysis

Maintaining clarity and consistency across chapters in both code and communication

title: 8  Emma Tupone
text:

Built strong skills in data cleaning, visualization, and statistical modeling

Developed the ability to interpret and communicate complex results

Gained experience working with diverse, real-world datasets

Strengthened reproducible research practices through integrated workflows

title: 8  Emma Tupone
text:
8.7 Looking Ahead
Emma leaves the course with increased confidence in her ability to independently explore and analyze data, select appropriate statistical methods, and communicate findings in a clear and professional manner. She is well-prepared to apply these skills in future research, coursework, and professional settings.

“This semester reinforced the value of persistence, curiosity, and attention to detail in research.”

title: Reproducible Research Using R: Student Work in Practice
text:
A Connected Learning Ecosystem
This volume is part of a broader ecosystem of open, reproducible teaching materials developed for this course.
At the core is the textbook, Reproducible Research Using R, which introduces the foundational concepts and technical skills needed to work with data in R.
Complementing this is Teaching Reproducible Research Using R, which focuses on implementation. This companion resource includes the assignments and workflows that structure the course and guide students through the practical application of those concepts.
Building on this foundation, students also conducted independent research projects, which were compiled into the NYC Open Data Student Gallery Book, presented at NYC Open Data Week 2026.
Together, these components form a complete pipeline:

Concepts and foundations (textbook)

Applied learning and assignments (teaching companion)

Student portfolios (this volume)

Independent research and publication (student gallery)

This structure reflects a deliberate approach to teaching reproducible research—one that emphasizes not only learning concepts, but applying them in meaningful, public-facing ways.

“I feel more confident and not scared to start coding something from scratch on my own.”

title: 3  Joyce Escatel-Flores

title: 4  Robert Hutto
text:
4.1 About the Author
Rob is a graduate student at Brooklyn College who developed a structured and thoughtful approach to data analysis over the course of the semester. His work reflects a transition from fragmented coding practices to a more intentional, reproducible workflow in R.

title: 4  Robert Hutto
text:
4.2 Learning Journey
At the start of the course, Rob had some prior exposure to RStudio through independent research, but his approach lacked organization and clarity. Much of his work involved copying and pasting code—often with limited understanding of how individual components functioned—resulting in scripts that were difficult to interpret or maintain.
Through consistent practice and guided assignments, he developed a more systematic approach to working with data. His portfolio demonstrates the ability to import, clean, and prepare datasets from multiple sources, conduct statistical analyses such as t-tests, ANOVAs, and regression models, and create clear visualizations using tables and graphs.
Just as importantly, he learned to structure his work in a way that is readable and interpretable by others, allowing both himself and collaborators to better understand analytical workflows.

title: 4  Robert Hutto
text:
4.3 Reflection & Growth
One of Rob’s most significant areas of growth was moving from simply executing code to truly understanding it. This shift enabled him to build more organized, transparent, and reproducible analyses.
He also developed a strong ability to identify and communicate meaningful patterns within data—transforming raw datasets into clear, interpretable stories. This skill represents a key step beyond technical execution and into applied data analysis.
A recurring challenge throughout the course was managing expectations around assignments. Tasks that initially appeared straightforward often required significant time to debug and refine, reinforcing the importance of patience and persistence in programming.

title: 4  Robert Hutto
text:
4.4 Advice for Future Students

Don’t underestimate seemingly simple tasks. Budget extra time for troubleshooting. What looks like a 30-minute assignment can easily become a multiple-hour debugging session—even with the help of AI. That’s part of the learning process.

title: 4  Robert Hutto
text:
4.5 Key Takeaways

Transitioned from unstructured coding to organized, reproducible workflows

Developed proficiency in data cleaning, analysis, and visualization

Improved ability to read, understand, and communicate code

Strengthened skills in extracting and explaining insights from data

5.1 About the Author
Isley is a graduate student at Brooklyn College whose work reflects a strong engagement with data analysis and reproducible research. Her portfolio represents the culmination of a semester of hands-on learning in R, showcasing both technical development and enthusiasm for applying these skills in future projects.

title: 5  Isley Jean-Pierre
text:
5.2 Learning Journey
This portfolio brings together a series of assignments completed as part of a course in Reproducible Psychology Research. Each section highlights a different stage of the data analysis process, from foundational R scripting to more advanced statistical techniques and reproducible workflows.
Over the course of the semester, Isley developed experience working with a range of analytical methods, including correlation analysis and ANOVA, while also building skills in creating and submitting work using R Markdown and Shiny. Her portfolio reflects not just individual assignments, but a continuous progression of learning and application.

title: 5  Isley Jean-Pierre
text:
5.3 Reflection & Growth
This project represents the culmination of Isley’s work throughout the semester and stands out as one of the most meaningful and engaging assignments in the course. It captures both the breadth of skills she developed and her ability to bring them together into a cohesive, reproducible body of work.
Each section of the portfolio tells the story of her growth—demonstrating how initial exposure to R evolved into the ability to perform analyses, build reproducible documents, and create interactive outputs.

title: 5  Isley Jean-Pierre
text:

Built a strong foundation in R programming and data analysis

Applied statistical methods such as correlation and ANOVA

Developed reproducible workflows using R Markdown

Gained experience creating interactive outputs with Shiny

Synthesized semester-long work into a cohesive portfolio

title: 5  Isley Jean-Pierre
text:
5.5 Looking Ahead
Isley is excited to apply the skills she developed in this course to future projects. Her portfolio reflects both her current abilities and her readiness to continue building on this foundation.

title: 6  Shannon Joyce
text:
6.1 About the Author
Shannon is a graduate student at Brooklyn College who developed a comprehensive skill set in data analysis and reproducible research over the course of the semester. Her work reflects a strong ability to move from raw data to meaningful insights using structured, reproducible workflows in R.

title: 6  Shannon Joyce
text:
6.2 Learning Journey
This portfolio is a compilation of assignments completed in a graduate course on Reproducible Psychological Research. Across these projects, Shannon demonstrates proficiency in writing and executing R scripts, cleaning and transforming real-world datasets, and conducting statistical analyses.
Her work spans the full data analysis pipeline—from data import and wrangling to modeling, visualization, and communication. She developed experience using tools from the :contentReferenceoaicite:0 to efficiently manipulate data, including filtering, grouping, summarizing, and joining datasets in preparation for analysis.

title: 6  Shannon Joyce
text:
6.3 Reflection & Growth
At the beginning of the course, Shannon had limited experience with R. Early efforts focused on building foundational skills and becoming comfortable working within the RStudio environment. By the end of the semester, she was able to independently complete full analytical workflows—moving from raw data to statistical modeling and clear interpretation.
A key area of growth was working with real datasets, which often required substantial cleaning and restructuring before analysis. Shannon developed skills in inspecting variables, recoding values, handling missing data, and reshaping datasets into formats suitable for analysis. These steps were essential in ensuring the accuracy and validity of her results.

title: 6  Shannon Joyce
text:
6.4 Analytical Skills & Techniques
Throughout the semester, Shannon gained experience applying a wide range of statistical methods, including:

t-tests for comparing group means

ANOVAs for analyzing differences across multiple groups

Correlation matrices for exploring relationships between variables

Regression models for prediction and explanation

She also learned to evaluate model outputs holistically, considering multiple variables and metrics rather than relying on a single statistic.

title: 6  Shannon Joyce
text:
6.5 Communication & Visualization
In addition to statistical analysis, Shannon strengthened her ability to communicate results clearly and effectively. She learned to translate statistical findings into plain language, connecting results back to the original research questions.
Her portfolio includes data visualizations created with :contentReferenceoaicite:1, as well as reproducible reports built using :contentReferenceoaicite:2. These tools allowed her to integrate code, output, and narrative into cohesive, shareable documents.
She also explored interactive data presentation through the development of a Shiny application.

title: 6  Shannon Joyce
text:
6.6 Key Takeaways

Built end-to-end data analysis workflows in R

Developed strong data cleaning and transformation skills

Applied a range of statistical and predictive modeling techniques

Strengthened ability to interpret and communicate results

Created reproducible reports and interactive applications

title: 6  Shannon Joyce
text:
6.7 Looking Ahead
Shannon now feels confident continuing to build on these skills in future academic and professional work. Her portfolio reflects both a strong technical foundation and the ability to apply data analysis to meaningful questions.

title: 7  Emma Tupone
text:
7.1 About the Author
Emma is a graduate student at Brooklyn College whose work reflects a strong foundation in psychological research and data analysis. Her portfolio demonstrates both technical proficiency and the ability to apply reproducible research practices to a wide range of real-world datasets.

title: 7  Emma Tupone
text:
7.2 Learning Journey
This portfolio presents a collection of data analysis projects completed as part of a graduate course in Reproducible Psychological Research. Across chapters, Emma applies reproducible workflows in R to diverse datasets, including public health records, socioeconomic indicators, sports analytics, and behavioral data.
Her work highlights core competencies in data cleaning, visualization, statistical inference, and predictive modeling, all integrated through reproducible reporting tools such as :contentReferenceoaicite:0 and Shiny.

title: 7  Emma Tupone
text:
7.3 Reflection & Growth
Over the course of the semester, Emma significantly deepened her understanding of both data analysis and research methodology. Through hands-on projects, she built skills in manipulating and cleaning data, visualizing patterns, and applying statistical techniques to answer meaningful research questions.
She developed proficiency using :contentReferenceoaicite:1 to create clear and informative visualizations, and gained experience with statistical methods including t-tests, ANOVAs, chi-square tests, and logistic regression. Beyond execution, she strengthened her ability to interpret results thoughtfully and connect findings to real-world implications.

title: 7  Emma Tupone
text:
7.4 Challenges & Problem Solving
Emma encountered several challenges throughout the semester, including:

Debugging complex R code, particularly when working with large datasets or interactive components

Interpreting nuanced statistical outputs such as residuals, effect sizes, and model diagnostics

Synthesizing insights across multiple assignments into cohesive conclusions

Addressing these challenges required persistence, iterative testing, and careful attention to both coding and statistical reasoning.

title: 7  Emma Tupone
text:
7.5 Achievements
Emma is especially proud of completing her Bookdown portfolio, which integrates multiple assignments into a cohesive and polished body of work. This project reflects her ability to combine reproducible code, clear visualizations, and well-structured interpretations.
Notable accomplishments include:

Developing interactive visualizations using Shiny

Conducting advanced analyses such as logistic regression and chi-square contribution analysis

Maintaining clarity and consistency across chapters in both code and communication

title: 7  Emma Tupone
text:
7.6 Key Takeaways

Built strong skills in data cleaning, visualization, and statistical modeling

Developed the ability to interpret and communicate complex results

Gained experience working with diverse, real-world datasets

Strengthened reproducible research practices through integrated workflows

title: 7  Emma Tupone
text:
7.7 Looking Ahead
Emma leaves the course with increased confidence in her ability to independently explore and analyze data, select appropriate statistical methods, and communicate findings in a clear and professional manner. She is well-prepared to apply these skills in future research, coursework, and professional settings.

“This semester reinforced the value of persistence, curiosity, and attention to detail in research.”

title: 8  Xinru Wang
text:
8.1 About the Author
Xinru is a graduate student at Brooklyn College who developed a strong foundation in data analysis and reproducible research over the course of the semester. Her work reflects both technical growth and an increasing confidence in applying R to real-world datasets.

title: 8  Xinru Wang
text:
8.2 Learning Journey
This portfolio brings together a series of applied data analysis projects developed using R and Quarto. Across these assignments, Xinru worked through the full data analysis process—from loading and cleaning data to statistical modeling, visualization, and interpretation.
Her work highlights key skills such as preparing datasets by handling missing values, recoding variables, and transforming data into usable formats. She also gained experience running statistical analyses to explore patterns and answer research questions, as well as creating visualizations that clearly communicate results.
Through the use of reproducible workflows, she learned how to connect code, output, and written interpretation into cohesive analytical reports.

title: 8  Xinru Wang
text:
8.3 Reflection & Growth
Over the course of the semester, Xinru became increasingly confident in her coding abilities. While she initially felt uncertain about her skill level, repeated practice and project-based learning helped her build a strong foundation in R.
One of the more challenging aspects of the course was understanding the logic behind functions—particularly when writing her own. This pushed her to think more deeply about how code is structured and how different components interact within an analysis.

title: 8  Xinru Wang
text:
8.4 Strengths & Achievements
Xinru is especially proud of the data visualizations she created. Being able to clearly communicate results through visual outputs—and share those insights with others—helped her recognize the practical value of her skills as a data analyst.
Her portfolio demonstrates the ability to move from raw data to meaningful, interpretable results while maintaining clarity and reproducibility throughout the process.

title: 8  Xinru Wang
text:
8.5 Key Takeaways

Developed end-to-end data analysis workflows in R

Built strong skills in data cleaning, transformation, and visualization

Gained experience with statistical modeling and interpretation

Strengthened understanding of reproducible reporting using Quarto

Increased confidence in independently learning new tools and techniques

title: 8  Xinru Wang
text:
8.6 Looking Ahead
With a solid foundation in place, Xinru plans to continue expanding her skills by exploring new R packages and applying her knowledge to additional datasets and research questions.

“Each chapter reflects a step in my learning process, showing how I moved from raw data to meaningful, interpretable results.”

title: 9  Laura Rose Werner
text:
9.1 About the Author
Laura is a graduate student at Brooklyn College whose work reflects a strong emphasis on reproducible research, statistical reasoning, and professional data communication. Her portfolio is designed not just as a collection of assignments, but as a polished analytical artifact suitable for academic and professional audiences.

title: 9  Laura Rose Werner
text:
9.2 Learning Journey
This portfolio presents a series of applied data analyses developed using Quarto. Across chapters, Laura works with diverse real-world datasets, including crime data, civic complaints, demographic indicators, sports analytics, and behavioral data.
Each project follows a consistent analytical workflow: - Data cleaning and preparation
- Visualization and exploration
- Statistical testing or modeling
- Clear interpretation of results
Her work demonstrates the ability to move systematically from raw data to meaningful, well-communicated insights.

title: 9  Laura Rose Werner
text:
9.3 Reflection & Growth
A defining feature of Laura’s work is her transition from assignment-based analysis to intentional, structured communication. This portfolio represents an evolution from an earlier Bookdown project into a refined Quarto-based publication.
Rather than simply completing analyses, she focused on improving: - clarity of interpretation
- organization of code and outputs
- reproducibility of results
- overall presentation and readability
This shift reflects a deeper understanding of what it means to produce high-quality analytical work.

title: 9  Laura Rose Werner
text:
9.4 Strengths & Achievements
Laura’s portfolio stands out for its breadth of applied analyses and its consistency in execution. Projects include:

NYC crime and shooting analyses

NYC 311 complaint data with interactive Shiny components

Income prediction using regression models

Streaming behavior using chi-square analysis

Sports analytics using NBA data

Across all projects, she demonstrates the ability to produce publication-quality tables and visualizations while maintaining fully reproducible workflows.

title: 9  Laura Rose Werner
text:
9.5 Key Takeaways

Developed strong reproducible workflows using Quarto

Strengthened statistical reasoning and interpretation

Built experience across multiple domains and datasets

Improved clarity and professionalism in analytical communication

Created a portfolio suitable for academic and professional review

title: 9  Laura Rose Werner
text:
9.6 What This Portfolio Demonstrates
Laura’s work highlights the ability to go beyond running statistical tests and instead focus on communicating meaningful, well-supported insights. Her portfolio emphasizes transparency, structure, and reproducibility—key components of modern data analysis and research.

“This portfolio demonstrates my ability to move beyond running statistical tests and toward communicating meaningful insights in a structured, reproducible way.”
